title: Clear one-page memo (no fluff)
content:
Rewrite the notes below into a clear, practical one-page memo.

Requirements:
- Keep the meaning; don’t add new claims.
- Use plain English and short sentences.
- Remove repetition and filler.
- If something is unclear or missing, insert a bracketed question like: [Need date], [Define KPI].
- Keep it to ~300–450 words.

Structure:
1) Title (specific)
2) Purpose (1–2 sentences)
3) Key points (bullets)
4) Decision / ask (what needs to happen next)
5) Next steps (3–5 bullets with owner + due date placeholders)

Notes to rewrite:
[paste notes here]

title: Landing Page Hero (Headline + Subhead + CTAs)
content:
Use the details below to write a landing page hero section that’s clear, specific, and conversion-focused.

Input
- Product/service: 
- Target customer: 
- Top problem they feel today: 
- Key outcome they want: 
- 3 main features (plain language): 
- Proof (metrics, logos, testimonials, years, or "none"): 
- Differentiator (why us vs alternatives): 
- Offer (free trial/demo/consultation/etc.): 
- Primary CTA text (if you have one): 
- Brand voice keywords (e.g., direct, calm, bold): 
- Must-avoid words/claims: 

Output (professional, concise)
1) 10 headline options (max 8 words each). Avoid hype; make the value concrete.
2) For the best 3 headlines, write matching subheads (1–2 sentences) that clarify who it’s for and what changes.
3) 2 CTA button options + 2 secondary CTA options.
4) One-sentence “micro-proof” line (use proof provided; if none, write a credibility-safe line without numbers).

Rules
- No clichés ("revolutionary", "game-changer", "world-class").
- Prefer specific nouns/verbs over adjectives.
- If any input is missing or vague, make the smallest reasonable assumption and keep claims conservative.

title: Friendly social media Q&A sticker
content:
Create a cohesive set of 6 square social media story stickers (1:1) designed for a dark-mode interface. Each sticker should feature a friendly, helpful micro-prompt for audience engagement (e.g., “Ask me anything”, “Quick poll”, “This or that”, “Drop a win”, “Need advice?”, “Hot take?”) using short text that is clearly readable.

Visual style: premium, cinematic, low-key lighting; cool/neutral color grading; deep navy/charcoal backgrounds with subtle grain and soft vignette; restrained saturation; gentle glow accents in cool cyan/blue-violet; minimal geometric shapes (rounded rectangles, thin outlines), soft shadows, and tasteful depth.

Composition: centered text with ample padding; one small icon per sticker (outline style) that matches the prompt (speech bubble, sliders, checkmarks, lightning bolt, etc.); consistent typography feel (clean modern sans, slightly condensed, high legibility). Keep a unified design system across all 6—same spacing, corner radius, icon stroke weight, and lighting.

Camera/texture cues: crisp vector-like edges blended with subtle realistic UI lighting; slight emboss/deboss effect; soft reflections as if viewed on a high-end OLED screen.

Avoid: bright white backgrounds, warm cozy daylight, busy patterns, cluttered gradients, heavy neon, playful cartoon vibes, random fonts.

Output: one image containing a 2x3 grid of the six stickers, evenly spaced, on a dark canvas; no logos or brand names; no watermarks; text must be spelled correctly and fully readable.

title: Draft a concise decision-driving email (clear ask, no pressure)
content:
Help me write a confident, structured email that drives a clear decision without sounding pushy.

Context (paste and keep what’s relevant):
- Recipient(s) + role(s):
- My role / team:
- Relationship / history in 1–2 lines:
- Topic / request:
- Why it matters (impact, deadline, risk):
- What I’ve already done:
- Constraints (budget, policy, scope, dependencies):
- Proposed path forward:
- Decision needed from them (be explicit):
- Options I can offer (up to 3):
- My recommended option and why:
- Timeline (preferred decision date + key dates):
- Links / docs to reference:
- Any sensitivities (politics, tone, legal, layoffs, etc.):

Requirements:
- Tone: confident and structured; calm, professional, and respectful.
- Keep it skimmable: short paragraphs, clear headers, and bullets where helpful.
- Lead with the ask in the first 2 sentences.
- Include a single, unambiguous call to action with a date/time.
- If information is missing, make reasonable assumptions and mark them as [ASSUMPTION].
- Avoid jargon, filler, and apology-heavy language.

Output:
1) Subject line options (5), varying urgency.
2) Email body.
3) A 1-sentence follow-up message if there’s no reply after 48 hours.
4) A version that is 30% shorter (same content, tighter).

Here is the raw input to use:
<<<
PASTE MY NOTES HERE
>>>

title: Convert product idea into a 1-page business pitch
content:
Take my rough product idea below and turn it into a crisp 1-page business pitch I can paste into a doc.

Requirements:
- Keep it modern, direct, and free of buzzwords.
- If something is unclear, make the smallest reasonable assumption and flag it as an assumption (don’t ask me questions first).
- Use short sections with punchy bullets.
- Focus on what an operator or investor would actually want to know.

Include these sections:
1) One-liner (what it is + who it’s for + outcome)
2) Problem (3 bullets) and why now (2 bullets)
3) Target customer (primary + secondary) + a concrete use case
4) Solution overview (what the user does in 5 steps)
5) Differentiation (3 clear points vs. common alternatives)
6) Business model (pricing idea + what drives expansion)
7) Go-to-market (first 3 channels + first 2 partnerships)
8) Metrics that matter (activation, retention, revenue, efficiency)
9) Risks & mitigations (top 3)
10) 30/60/90-day plan (specific actions, not goals)

My rough idea (paste and work from this):
[IDEA]

title: Rough product idea turned into a simple UI concept and layout plan
content:
I’m designing a new feature/page. Take my rough description and turn it into a clear, usable UI concept.

Input (messy notes):
[PASTE IDEA HERE]

Output:
1) One-sentence purpose of the screen
2) Primary user and their top goal
3) Key states to design (empty, loading, error, success, first-time)
4) Layout plan for desktop and mobile (describe sections top-to-bottom)
5) Core components list (buttons, tables, cards, forms, filters, etc.) with short notes on behavior
6) Primary and secondary CTAs (exact button labels)
7) 5 microcopy lines I can paste in (headings, helper text, empty-state text, error message)
8) Top 3 usability risks + how to reduce them

Constraints:
- Keep it lightweight: avoid long explanations.
- Prefer familiar patterns over novelty.
- Assume accessibility basics (contrast, keyboard focus, clear labels).

If anything critical is missing, ask up to 3 targeted questions, then proceed with reasonable assumptions.

title: Hero image: abstract AI network orb
content:
Generate a cinematic hero image for a dark product interface.

Subject: an abstract “AI network orb” made of thin metallic filaments and subtle glowing nodes, hovering above a matte black surface.
Composition: centered, symmetrical, plenty of negative space for headline text; shallow depth of field; slight top-down 3/4 angle.
Lighting: low-key studio lighting with a cool rim light and soft fill; gentle specular highlights; soft shadows; no harsh reflections.
Color grade: cool neutrals (charcoal, slate, deep navy) with restrained cyan/blue accents; minimal saturation.
Background: dark gradient with faint atmospheric haze; no clutter, no text, no logos.
Style: premium, high-end tech aesthetic; photoreal/CG hybrid; ultra-clean.
Output: 16:9, high resolution, crisp details, noise-free.

title: Coding workspace hero image (friendly, calm, premium)
content:
Cinematic hero image of a modern coding workspace designed to sit inside a dark product UI: a laptop on a matte black desk with a code editor open (clean syntax highlighting, no readable brand names), subtle terminal pane, and a small floating tooltip-style UI bubble that feels friendly and helpful (minimal rounded rectangle, soft glow, no text or only abstract glyphs). Nighttime studio setting with low-key, directional light from the left, soft shadows, gentle rim light on the laptop edges, and a faint cool neon accent (deep navy + charcoal + cool gray with a hint of blue/purple). Composition: 3/4 angle from slightly above, medium close-up, shallow depth of field, background falls into a dark blur with a few out-of-focus bokeh lights. Materials: anodized aluminum laptop, textured keycaps, microfiber desk mat, subtle cable management. Mood: calm, approachable, focused, premium. Color grading: restrained saturation, cool/neutral, high dynamic range but not harsh.

Camera look: 50mm lens, f/1.8, ISO 400, 1/60, crisp foreground detail, soft atmospheric falloff.

Negative prompts: bright daylight, warm orange cast, cozy lifestyle scene, white background, clutter, messy cables, readable logos/brands, meme imagery, overly vibrant colors, cartoon style, excessive bloom, busy UI text, watermarks.

title: From messy to-do list to a realistic daily plan (time-blocked)
content:
Take my raw to-do list and turn it into a realistic plan for today.

Inputs I’ll paste below:
1) My to-do list (messy is fine)
2) Any fixed appointments + times
3) My available work window (start/end) + timezone

What I want back:
- A prioritized list using: Must / Should / Could
- A time-blocked schedule that fits my work window (include short breaks)
- For each Must item: the next physical action (one sentence)
- If the list doesn’t fit: clearly state what gets deferred and why
- A 2-minute “start now” step that gets me moving immediately

Rules:
- Be strict about time; don’t assume I can do 8 hours of deep work.
- Default to 60–90 minute focus blocks.
- If any task is vague, rewrite it into an actionable version.

Here are my details:
[PASTE TO-DO LIST]

Appointments:
[PASTE APPOINTMENTS]

Work window/timezone:
[PASTE HOURS + TIMEZONE]

title: Research question turned into a tight literature map + synthesis outline
content:
I’m starting a research project and I want you to act like a research lead who’s great at scoping, narrowing, and building a defensible literature narrative.

Research question (paste):
[...]
Context & constraints:
- Domain/field: [...]
- Audience (advisor, product team, policy, etc.): [...]
- Output length target: [...]
- Time horizon (last X years vs seminal + recent): [...]
- Method preference (theory, empirical, mixed): [...]

Deliverables (be rigorous and specific):
1) Reframe the question
- Provide 3 alternative formulations: (a) broad, (b) fundable/publishable, (c) narrow + testable.
- For each, list: key constructs, assumed mechanisms, likely confounders, and what would count as a meaningful contribution.

2) Conceptual map
- Create a structured “literature map” with 4–7 clusters (schools of thought / mechanisms / methods).
- For each cluster: what it explains, what it misses, common measures/proxies, typical datasets/contexts, and 2–3 seminal anchors + what made them influential (no need for exact citations if unsure; describe the paper type precisely so I can find it).

3) Search strategy (so I can replicate)
- Give a query bank with Boolean strings for Google Scholar / Scopus:
  - core query
  - mechanism-focused queries
  - methods/identification queries
  - adjacent-field queries
- Include synonym sets for each key construct and “exclusion” terms to reduce irrelevant hits.

4) Inclusion/exclusion rules
- Define screening criteria (population/context, study design, measures, language, years).
- Add a bias checklist: publication bias, survivorship, measurement error, reverse causality, omitted variables, p-hacking, generalizability.

5) Evidence table template
- Produce a copy-paste table schema with columns for: claim, study type, sample/context, identification strategy, measures, effect direction/magnitude (if known), robustness checks, limitations, and how it connects to my question.

6) Synthesis plan
- Write a synthesis outline (not a full essay) that:
  - states the debate(s)
  - compares clusters
  - identifies contradictions and why they occur (method, context, definitions)
  - ends with a clean “gap → approach → expected contribution” arc
- Provide 2 competing theoretical explanations and what evidence would discriminate between them.

7) Next-step recommendations
- Propose 3 concrete project paths:
  - one low-risk / incremental
  - one medium-risk with a stronger contribution
  - one high-risk / high-upside
- For each: minimal viable dataset or data collection plan, analysis approach, and main threats to validity.

Style requirements:
- Modern, direct, and structured.
- Make uncertainty explicit (use confidence levels) and never invent citations. If you’re not sure about a specific paper, describe it so I can locate it.
- If crucial details are missing, ask up to 5 targeted questions before proceeding, otherwise make reasonable assumptions and label them.

title: Write a high-converting landing page section (headline, subhead, bullets, CTA)
content:
Create copy for a landing page section that sells the offer below. Keep it clear, practical, and skimmable (no hype, no buzzwords, no fluff). Write in plain English.

Offer details:
- Product/service: [what it is]
- Target audience: [who it’s for]
- Primary pain point: [what they struggle with]
- Desired outcome: [what they want]
- Key features (3–6): [feature list]
- Proof (optional): [numbers, logos, testimonial snippet]
- Objections to address: [price/time/trust/etc.]
- Call to action: [button text] + [what happens next]

Output (in this exact order):
1) 10 headline options (max 8 words each). Focus on the outcome and the audience.
2) 5 subheadline options (1–2 sentences each). Add clarity and specificity.
3) One final recommended headline + subheadline pair (choose the best combination and state why in one short sentence).
4) Benefit bullets: 6 bullets total.
   - 3 “Outcome” bullets (end result)
   - 3 “How it works” bullets (mechanism)
5) Objection-handling mini-FAQ: 4 Q&As, each answer max 2 sentences.
6) Primary CTA: 3 button text options + one supporting line under the button (microcopy).

Constraints:
- Write at an 8th-grade reading level.
- Use concrete words; avoid vague claims like “boost,” “revolutionary,” “next-level.”
- If info is missing, make reasonable assumptions and mark them with (Assumption: …).

title: Research Learning Ops: Rubric + Quiz Generator with Mastery Routing
content:
Build a reusable learning-ops system for teaching a research skill (topic can vary). Output must be operational artifacts (tables, rules, item banks), not an essay.

Input (fill in):
- Learner level: [high school | undergrad | grad | professional]
- Research skill focus: [e.g., literature search, hypothesis design, operationalization, survey design, causal inference, qualitative coding, replication]
- Domain/context: [e.g., public health, product analytics, education, finance]
- Time available: [minutes or sessions]
- Allowed tools: [none | Google Scholar | PubMed | Scopus | Zotero | R | Python | Excel]
- Constraints: [e.g., no paywalled sources, must use primary studies, limited sample size]

Deliverables (produce in this order):
1) Learning objective map
- 3–6 measurable objectives (verb + observable evidence).
- For each: prerequisites, common misconceptions, and an example “acceptable evidence” artifact.

2) Rubric (analytic)
- Create a rubric table with 4 performance levels: Novice / Developing / Proficient / Advanced.
- Rows must cover: problem framing, source quality, method fit, validity threats/bias, synthesis/logic, transparency/reproducibility.
- Include explicit observable criteria per cell (no vague adjectives).
- Add a scoring rule: weights that sum to 100 and a pass threshold.

3) Question bank with tagging (15 items)
- Generate 15 assessment items: 6 multiple-choice, 5 short-answer, 4 scenario-based.
- Each item must include:
  - ID
  - Objective tag(s)
  - Difficulty (1–5)
  - Format
  - Prompt
  - Correct answer / key
  - Rationale (1–2 lines)
  - Common wrong answer(s) + why they’re wrong
- Ensure at least 5 items directly test validity threats/bias and at least 4 items test synthesis across sources.

4) Mastery routing logic
- Create a simple decision system that routes learners based on performance:
  - If overall score >= [X] and no objective below “Proficient” → “Advance” track
  - If score in middle band or any objective below “Developing” → “Targeted practice” track
  - If score low or multiple objectives “Novice” → “Re-teach” track
- Specify X and bands using your rubric weights.
- Output as:
  - a rules table (conditions → action)
  - plus pseudocode.

5) Targeted practice generator (templates)
- For each objective, create:
  - 2 micro-drills (5–10 min)
  - 1 retrieval practice prompt
  - 1 error-correction prompt that uses the learner’s wrong answer.

6) Instructor checklist + QA
- A 10-point checklist for instructors/graders to keep scoring consistent.
- Include 5 QA tests to detect bad items (ambiguity, multiple correct answers, mis-tagging, tool dependence, domain leakage).

Constraints on your output:
- Keep everything concise and scannable.
- Use tables where helpful.
- Avoid referencing external documents; if you cite examples, invent them realistically.
- If any input is missing, make sensible assumptions and state them in one line at the top before the deliverables.
